Name: jacobian_based_layer_skipping
Title: Jacobian-Based Layer Skipping for Latency-Aware Transformer Inference
Short Hypothesis: Layer-skipping decisions in transformers can be dynamically optimized by approximating the local curvature of the loss landscape using the Frobenius norm of the Jacobian matrix, enabling latency-aware inference without sacrificing accuracy.
Related Work: Existing methods for layer-skipping in transformers often rely on heuristic thresholds or static policies, which fail to adapt to input-specific dynamics. Recent work on training-free inference frameworks, such as Spectral Quantization Routing and Geometric Signal Separation, has introduced geometric diagnostics for dynamic compute allocation. However, these methods do not explicitly link layer-skipping decisions to the optimization landscape's geometry. Our proposal bridges this gap by leveraging the Jacobian matrix to approximate local curvature, providing a theoretically grounded and computationally efficient mechanism for latency-aware inference.
Abstract: Transformer models have achieved remarkable success across various domains, but their computational demands remain a significant challenge. Layer-skipping offers a promising solution to reduce inference latency, but existing methods often rely on heuristic thresholds or static policies, failing to adapt to input-specific dynamics. We propose a training-free framework for dynamic layer-skipping in transformers, where skipping decisions are optimized based on the local curvature of the loss landscape. Specifically, we approximate the curvature using the Frobenius norm of the Jacobian matrix, enabling efficient computation and dynamic adaptation to input complexity. We theoretically ground our approach by linking the Frobenius norm to PAC-Bayes generalization bounds under perturbation. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across diverse benchmarks, our method achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic layer-skipping baselines. By replacing qualitative fragility claims with formalized risk bounds, our framework provides a transparent, reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Measure the correlation between the Frobenius norm of the Jacobian matrix and layer-skipping sensitivity across different layers of a transformer model. Use a diverse set of inputs from standard benchmarks (e.g., GLUE, WILDS) and compute the Frobenius norm for each layer. Evaluate sensitivity by comparing the accuracy drop when skipping each layer.

Experiment 2: Implement a dynamic layer-skipping policy based on the Frobenius norm threshold. Test this policy on single-GPU setups across various benchmarks, comparing inference latency and accuracy against static layer-skipping baselines. Use metrics like throughput (tokens/second) and accuracy retention to evaluate performance.

Experiment 3: Validate the PAC-Bayes generalization bounds by measuring the model's robustness to adversarial perturbations under different Frobenius norm thresholds. Use adversarial datasets (e.g., TextFooler) and measure accuracy retention and inference latency.

Experiment 4: Conduct ablation studies to evaluate the impact of different Jacobian approximation methods (e.g., randomized SVD vs. full computation) on the efficiency and accuracy of the layer-skipping policy.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The Frobenius norm approximation may introduce computational overhead, potentially offsetting the latency gains from layer-skipping.
The method assumes that the local curvature of the loss landscape is a reliable proxy for layer-skipping sensitivity, which may not hold for all inputs or architectures.
The approach is limited to transformer-based models and may not generalize to other architectures.
The theoretical bounds rely on assumptions about the perturbation distribution, which may not always align with real-world conditions.
